behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix.
behaviors.justification: CANDIDATE-TEST-CHECK: Unit test of `sortByNumericPrefix(files)` catches numeric-vs-lexicographic bugs (e.g., `10_...` before `2_...`). Integration test (real dir + runner) also catches this, but lower level already catches the concrete failure in this behavior. MOCK-MISS: failure is in ordering logic of one function, not inherently a module handoff bug. REAL-INFRA/LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. REJECTION-GATE: rejected Integration because behavior text focuses on ordering rule itself ("ascending numeric order determined by filename prefix"), which is fully checkable at function boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Feed an intentionally unsorted filename list (`001_init.sql`, `010_add.sql`, `002_users.sql`) into the ordering function and assert returned order is numeric ascending by prefix.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run.
behaviors.justification: CANDIDATE-TEST-CHECK: Unit test of "filter pending" logic catches pure set-difference mistakes, but misses real failure where first run writes `_migrations` and second run reads it through repo/service wiring. Integration test with runner + migration store catches state handoff across runs. REAL-INFRA: not required if using in-process DB/fake repo; behavior is idempotency orchestration. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) no required OS/subprocess/network for this specific failure boundary, (b) no AI output, (c) no agentic chain. REJECTION-GATE: rejected System because behavior text is about "already been applied ... subsequent run" (cross-module persisted state), not DB engine/network edge behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Run runner twice against same test store: first run applies `001.sql`; second run executes zero SQL bodies for `001.sql` while `_migrations` still records a single application.

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted.
behaviors.justification: CANDIDATE-TEST-CHECK: Integration test with mocked transaction manager can verify "stop after failure" control flow, but can falsely pass while real DB autocommit/transaction behavior leaves partial writes. System test with real Postgres transaction semantics catches rollback reality plus stop-on-failure. REAL-INFRA: yes, failure boundary includes actual DB rollback semantics. MOCK-HIDE: yes, mocks usually assume ideal rollback. INTEGRATION-BRAKE(a): yes -> System.
behaviors.test_description: Using real Postgres, execute migrations where `001.sql` inserts a row then fails mid-file, and `002.sql` would insert another row. Assert row from `001.sql` is absent (rolled back), `002.sql` was not applied, and only prior successful migrations remain recorded.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances.
behaviors.justification: CANDIDATE-TEST-CHECK: Integration test with single-process faked locking can miss real race windows between separate processes. System test with two real processes against same Postgres catches duplicate-apply race, lock timing, and unique-constraint contention. REAL-INFRA: yes (separate processes + real DB concurrency). MOCK-HIDE: yes, deterministic fakes hide timing issues. INTEGRATION-BRAKE(a): yes -> System.
behaviors.test_description: Spawn two runner processes concurrently against one Postgres instance and same migrations directory; assert each migration’s side effects appear once and `_migrations` has one record per migration with no duplicates.

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the `_migrations` table is created before any migration file is applied.
behaviors.justification: CANDIDATE-TEST-CHECK: Unit test of call ordering in an orchestrator can pass while missing wiring defects between bootstrap and apply modules. Integration test with runner + migration store catches real initialization handoff/order. System with real Postgres is stronger but not strictly required to detect this ordering bug. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) no required OS/subprocess behavior for the order rule itself, (b) no AI output, (c) no agentic chain. REJECTION-GATE: rejected System because behavior text is specifically ordering ("table is created before any migration file is applied"), not DB-engine-specific concurrency/network semantics.
behaviors.test_description: Against a fresh test store, run startup and capture executed operations: assert `_migrations` table creation occurs before first migration execution, and first run succeeds without "relation does not exist" errors.
